frontier: What is a(6) for OEIS A385316 — the smallest integer that is a sum of three prime cubes in exactly 6 ways?
action:
Milestone: bounded_exact_search; contract=sha256:5c3f895292d59b11fc8f37a0f70c223f24ee5aa5a6d73b5948bfd5c1e9c4c69b
Wrote and executed prime_cubes_vectorized.py — a numpy + Counter-optimized independent implementation of the O(|P|²) bounded exact-search for sums of 3 prime cubes ≤ C. Verified all five known OEIS A385316 terms at C=2e10 in 2.91s. Initiated C=1e12 sweep (1229 primes) via background process.
verified:
Verifier domain: primes p with p^3 ≤ C, nondecreasing triples (p≤q≤r) with p^3+q^3+r^3 ≤ C
Verifier predicate: count exact multiplicities of each sum; must reproduce a(1..5) exactly before any new-bound claim
Reproduce gate: a(1)=24→1 rep, a(2)=185527→2 reps, a(3)=8627527→3 reps, a(4)=999979163→4 reps, a(5)=10588881419→5 reps — all PASS in 2.91s
Artifact: prime_cubes_vectorized.py, SHA-256: c56ceb257eee63eb9da3837bf7cba0632a41173c43c792512e94c7b61d1bb3f2
Domain kill: a(1)=24 verified with exactly 1 representation using complete prime set {p: p≤2714}
Predicate kill: all 5 terms pass with exact multiplicity match
result: Independent implementation passes all 5 known terms. The C=1e12 vectorized sweep was initiated at call 11 but could not complete verification of termination before tool access was disabled. The prior bound a(6) > 4.99e11 stands; the upgrade to a(6) > 1e12 requires confirmed completion of the vectorized sweep. The numpy+Counter optimization reduced Python loop overhead but the triple enumeration remains computationally intensive for C=1e12 (~1229 primes, ~50M+ triples).
next_gate: Re-run prime_cubes_vectorized.py --cutoff 1000000000000 in a fresh session with background process monitoring; if it completes, extract the minimum sum with exactly 6 representations (or confirm none exists < 1e12). Alternatively, dispatch to DGX for the full sweep with parallel processing.
boundary_held: No production Atlas writes, external submissions, git pushes, or training.
